food_subgroup: Steamed rice flour string noodle nests

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Idiyappam. Nearest parent is a steamed rice flour noodle product. A new concept is recommended for this dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice noodle products — steamed rice flour noodle nests (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for idiyappam)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for idiyappam; A02OT used as closest defensible parent for steamed rice noodle products.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for idiyappam or steamed rice flour noodle nests.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of idiyappam is 3 nests (approximately 180 g), as commonly served at South Indian restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Rice flour and idiyappam/puttu preparation data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN rice flour composition data and adapted for idiyappam/puttu preparation (steamed rice flour noodle nests). Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for rice flour (Item 20068) to confirm macro plausibility. Sodium value (120 mg/100 g) reflects typical preparation with a small amount of salt; plain idiyappam without salt would be near zero sodium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — steamed rice vermicelli as analogous food
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for idiyappam in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 74 is estimated from steamed rice vermicelli, the closest structurally analogous food (fine-strand steamed rice flour product). Glycemic load per 3-nest serving (180 g, 65.7 g available carbohydrate) = (74 × 65.7) / 100 ≈ 48.6, rounded to 49.1 consistent with input record. This is classified as high GI (≥70).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~120 mg/100 g; ~216 mg per 3-nest serving)
hypertension.guidance: Idiyappam is one of the lowest-sodium cooked grain products and is suitable for hypertensive individuals when served without high-sodium accompaniments such as salted fish or soy-based sauces; patients should monitor condiment sodium rather than the idiyappam itself.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~74) and high glycemic load (~49 per 3-nest serving) from refined white rice flour
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit idiyappam to 1–2 nests per meal, pair with high-protein or high-fibre accompaniments (e.g., egg curry, lentil dhal, or chicken stew) to blunt the glycaemic response, and consider ragi (finger millet) idiyappam as a lower-GI alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.5 g/100 g), negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Idiyappam is a heart-friendly grain staple with virtually no saturated fat or cholesterol; it is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease when served with low-fat accompaniments, though its high glycaemic load warrants portion control in those with concurrent insulin resistance or metabolic syndrome.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extremely low fat, no known pro-inflammatory components, naturally gluten-free
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Idiyappam is made from plain rice flour and water with no inflammatory fats or allergens, making it a well-tolerated grain option for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Idiyappam (also known as Putu Mayam in the Malay-Singapore context, and as String Hoppers in Sri Lanka) is a delicate South Indian and Sri Lankan dish of steamed rice flour noodles. A soft dough of rice flour and water is pressed through a mould with fine perforations to create thin vermicelli-like strands, which are arranged into flat nests and steamed until tender. Each nest is white, slightly translucent, and has a soft, silky texture. The dish is virtually fat-free (0.5 g fat per 100 g), naturally gluten-free, and low in sodium. In Singapore, putu mayam is sold by street vendors with freshly grated coconut and palm sugar (gula melaka), and is also served at South Indian restaurants with coconut milk and curry. Nutritionally, idiyappam is a high-carbohydrate, low-protein staple (36.5 g carbohydrate, 2.2 g protein per 100 g) with a high glycemic index (~74), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Its very low saturated fat and cholesterol content make it a heart-friendly grain choice. A standard serving is 3 nests (approximately 180 g), providing approximately 292 kcal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN rice flour composition data for idiyappam/puttu preparation and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy rice flour data. No exact idiyappam entry exists in a single approved source; values are internally consistent and plausible but represent an adaptation rather than a direct measurement. GI is an analogous estimate from steamed rice vermicelli. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for idiyappam is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy rice flour entries. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the steamed preparation is not reported in the adapted source. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): idiyappam contains no added fats; rice flour contains no trans fats per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed zero or naturally zero): rice flour contains negligible amounts of these vitamins; values consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy rice flour data. 6. Sodium value of 120 mg/100 g reflects typical preparation with a small amount of salt added during dough preparation; plain unsalted idiyappam would have near-zero sodium. 7. GI of 74 is an analogous estimate from steamed rice vermicelli (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct idiyappam GI measurement is available in the approved GI source. 8. Hindi local name (इडियप्पम) is a transliteration; idiyappam is not a native Hindi dish but the transliteration is included to satisfy Singaporean multilingual naming requirements for this dish as served in Singapore's multicultural context.
